nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; typically found in sandy bottoms and rubble near coral reefs, often buried with only the head exposed. Occurs at depths of approximately 1–67 m.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on small fish and crustaceans. Wild individuals consume small fish; captive specimens have been known to accept live feeder fish, ghost shrimp, and crab, and some individuals can be transitioned to frozen silversides or other meaty foods offered via feeding tongs. New arrivals frequently go on prolonged hunger strikes; patience and variety are generally advised. Feeding every 2–3 days is typically recommended once eating is established.

avoidWith: small fish that fit in mouth

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Bernard DUPONT, taken at Wori, Manado, Sulawesi, Indonesia. Shows adult male (blue phase). High-resolution, clearly identified species.
Product hero image from LiveAquaria listing for Blue Ribbon Eel. Clean composition typical of retailer photography; shows blue adult male phase.

summary: The Blue Ribbon Eel is the striking adult male color phase of Rhinomuraena quaesita — vivid electric blue with a yellow snout — and is the same species as the Black Ribbon Eel (see sw-fish-017). Generally considered one of the most challenging marine eels to keep, requiring expert-level care and a very secure, well-decorated tank.
careNotes:
The Blue Ribbon Eel (Rhinomuraena quaesita) is a protandrous hermaphrodite: juveniles are jet black with a yellow dorsal fin; upon reaching approximately 65–80 cm they typically become males (the vivid blue phase with yellow snout and jaw documented here); upon reaching 85 cm or more many individuals transition to female, becoming predominantly yellow. This entry covers the blue adult male phase; see also sw-fish-017 (black-ribbon-eel) which documents the juvenile black phase of the same species. The reviewer may wish to consolidate these two entries.

Water parameters generally advised: temperature 23–27°C (sources ranged 22–29°C; averaged), pH 8.1–8.4 (consistent across sources), salinity SG 1.021–1.025. These parameters are typical marine reef values.

Tank setup: a deep sandy substrate (10–15 cm) is widely recommended so the eel can burrow. Caves, rock overhangs, or buried PVC pipe should also be provided. A tight-fitting lid or light-diffuser panel is essential — ribbon eels are adept escape artists and can push through surprisingly small gaps. A tank length of at least 150 cm is advisable for full-grown adults.

Feeding tends to be the primary husbandry challenge. Wild-caught individuals often refuse food initially, sometimes for weeks. Live feeder fish or ghost shrimp are typically used to initiate feeding; gradual transition to frozen silversides, krill, or other meaty foods on feeding tongs is generally attempted once the eel is established. Using feeding tongs is strongly advised — the eel has poor eyesight and can deliver a serious bite; wounds carry infection risk.

Capture method concerns: many individuals in the trade have been collected with cyanide, which causes internal damage that can manifest as refusal to eat and early death. Low survival rates in captivity (often 1–2 years vs. up to 20 years in the wild) are frequently attributed to collection stress, cyanide exposure, and feeding difficulties. Some well-cared-for individuals have been reported to survive longer when feeding is successfully established.

Min tank size disagreement across sources: LiveAquaria 100 gal (378 L), Reeflings 300 L (79 gal), Blue Zoo Aquatics 75 gal (284 L). Averaged to approximately 320 L; reviewer may prefer the higher LiveAquaria figure given the species' adult length.

Adult size: all sources agree on approximately 50 in (127 cm) for males; FishBase records a maximum of 130 cm total length. The blue (male) phase is typically 65–94 cm; the yellow (female) phase can reach 130 cm.

Lifespan: has been reported to live up to 20 years in the wild; captive longevity is typically far shorter, often 1–2 years, with occasional reports of longer survival under expert care.
breedingNotes: Has not been bred in captivity. The species is a protandrous hermaphrodite — individuals hatch as males (or juveniles of indeterminate sex) and may transition to female as they grow. In the wild, spawning takes place in open water. No successful captive spawning protocols are documented.

additional.name: Blue Zoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (50 in), care level (expert), reef compatibility (with caution), temperament (semi-aggressive), tank size (75 gal), and diet. Note: the original secondary source URL in the manifest was labeled 'FishBase' but resolves to Blue Zoo Aquatics; recorded under its actual name.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Muraenidae, Anguilliformes), native range (Indo-Pacific, East Africa to French Polynesia, south Japan to New Caledonia), maximum size 130 cm, temperature range 24.6–28.8°C, habitat (reef-associated, sandy/rubble substrate, 1–67 m depth), and protandrous hermaphrodite behavior.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (23–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (SG 1.022–1.025), tank size (300 L / 79 gal), adult size (1.2 m). This source rated care level as 'moderate to easy' — outlier vs. all other sources which rate it expert; not used for careLevel field. Used for numerical averaging.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Ribbon eel
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy, native range, color phase descriptions (black juvenile, blue male, yellow female), size (males 65–94 cm, females up to 130 cm), lifespan up to 20 years in the wild. Not a husbandry source; used for taxonomy and natural history only.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented. Protandrous hermaphrodite; juveniles mature as males then may transition to female. Spawning occurs in open water in the wild.
